title: GI stasis in rabbits
subtitle: Recognising and responding to the gut slowdown that kills rabbits within hours.
excerpt: Gastrointestinal stasis is the leading cause of sudden death in domestic rabbits. The gut stops moving, gas builds up, and the rabbit goes from normal to critical in under 24 hours. This covers the early signs, what to do immediately, and when a vet call is the only viable option.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Rabbits (2019). House Rabbit Society, GI Stasis educational resource. Smallholder Magazine, rabbit-health features.

content.text: will be dead within 24 to 48 hours without intervention in most cases. The challenge is recognising it early enough to act. The earliest signs are subtle: a rabbit that eats hay less enthusiastically than usual, fewer droppings in the cage, and a rabbit that sits hunched rather than stretched out. By the time the rabbit is clearly uncomfortable, gas has already built up and the window is narrower.
Check the litter tray or cage floor: normal droppings are firm, round, and regular. Fewer droppings than usual, smaller droppings, or no droppings for more than 12 hours is an early warning.
left in the cage rather than eaten indicate the rabbit is not engaging in normal digestive behaviour. A rabbit that refuses its favourite food is abnormal; rabbits are consistent feeders.
Remove pellets and keep only hay and water available. Gentle movement stimulates

: take the rabbit out and let it move on a flat surface for 15 minutes per hour. Gently massage the abdomen in a circular motion for two to three minutes at a time. A rabbit in early stasis that is still moving and showing some interest in hay can sometimes recover with this approach within six to eight hours. Keep the rabbit warm: stasis commonly accompanies chilling.

A rabbit that is grinding its teeth (bruxism), has a visibly bloated abdomen, or has not produced any droppings for 12 hours and is not responding to movement is in acute stasis. This is a vet visit, not a wait-and-see. Veterinary treatment includes gut motility drugs, pain relief, and subcutaneous fluids; the outcome is significantly better when treatment starts within 12 hours of symptom onset.

attrs.body: GI stasis is almost always preventable with correct feeding. A rabbit's diet should be 80 to 90% unlimited grass hay (Timothy, meadow, or orchard); pellets are a supplement, not the base. The fibre in hay is the only thing that maintains gut motility. A rabbit fed primarily on pellets will develop stasis faster than one with constant hay access.
attrs.title: Prevention: hay above everything
